sound_type: pure sustained trill thermometer -- one of the purest tones in nature, crystalline sustained trill, chirp rate correlates with temperature (Dolbear's Law)
ecosystem: Shrub. Low trees, herbaceous plants, hedgerows. Elevated calling positions on vegetation.
season: Late summer through autumn. Peak calling July through October.

coordinates.description: Reference for shrub habitat in the PNW, Washington. Snowy tree crickets in shrubs and low vegetation throughout lowlands.

taxonomy_note.description: GRYLLIDAE RETURNS -- first at degree 87 (cricket chorus, dense rhythmic chirping, E=4, dry meadow). Returns at degree 98 (tree cricket song, pure sustained trill, E=3, shrub). Same family, different species, radically different acoustic character.
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Oecanthus from Greek oikos ('house') + anthos ('flower') -- living among plants. fultoni named after entomologist. Common name 'snowy tree cricket' for pale coloration. 'Thermometer cricket' for temperature correlation.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Oecanthus -- tree crickets. Small, pale, delicate crickets that live on vegetation. Known for remarkably pure tones and temperature-correlated chirp rates.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Orthoptera -- continuing. Gryllidae (87, 98) and Tettigoniidae (88). Three Orthoptera entries. Crickets, katydids, grasshoppers.
taxonomy_note.class_note: Insecta -- eleven insect entries (87-98, with gaps for non-insects at 96-97). The insect era extends through degree 98.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Gryllidae returns at degree 98. First at degree 87. Same family, different species, different acoustic character. Within-family diversity.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 2000-4000 Hz. Species-dependent. Snowy tree cricket approximately 2500-3500 Hz at typical summer temperatures.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: Near the fundamental. Minimal harmonic content. Nearly sinusoidal waveform.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Sustained trill. Continuous stridulation. NOT a series of chirps -- a continuous trill.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Individual trill: several seconds. Calling: continuous for hours through the night.
song_metrics.note_structure: Single sustained trill. Continuous. No discrete chirps (unlike field crickets). The trill IS the call.
song_metrics.note_types: Sustained pure trill (mating call). Remarkably clean tone. Minimal harmonic content.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: No phrase structure. Continuous trill with occasional brief pauses. Multiple individuals often synchronize trills.
song_metrics.timbre: Crystalline. Pure. Clean. Nearly sinusoidal. The purest natural tone in the engine. No buzz, no rasp, no harmonic complexity. A biological tuning fork.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=3. Pure but gentle. Quieter than field cricket chorus (87, E=4). Clearly audible in still night air. Crystalline clarity compensates for moderate volume.
song_metrics.tempo: No tempo. Continuous sustained trill. Chirp rate within the trill is temperature-dependent (Dolbear's Law).
song_metrics.context: MATING CALL. Males call from shrubs and low vegetation to attract females. Late summer through autumn. Night calling. The pure tone carries through still air.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Oecanthus fultoni found across North America. PNW populations call in summer and early autumn. Chirp rate varies with local temperature.
song_metrics.key_property: PURE SUSTAINED TRILL THERMOMETER -- the 98th acoustic element (species side). One of the purest tones in nature. Nearly sinusoidal. Crystalline. E=3. Gryllidae RETURNS (first at 87). Dolbear's Law: chirp rate = temperature. Shrub habitat. Thirty-eighth consecutive original CSV.

physical_description.overall: Small, delicate cricket. 12-15 mm body length. Pale green to white ('snowy'). Long antennae. Transparent wings held flat over body. Lives on vegetation, not on the ground.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 98: (1) PALE/WHITE COLORATION -- 'snowy' appearance. (2) TRANSPARENT WINGS -- held flat over body. (3) FOUND ON VEGETATION -- shrubs, not ground. (4) LONG ANTENNAE -- longer than body. (5) PURE SUSTAINED TRILL -- diagnostic call.
physical_description.skin_detail: Thin, pale exoskeleton. Often nearly translucent. Pale green to white. Wings with visible venation. Delicate overall appearance -- much more slender than field crickets.
physical_description.dental_detail: Mandibles for feeding on plant material, aphids, and other small insects. Omnivorous.
physical_description.field_id: Small, pale, delicate cricket found on shrubs and low vegetation (not on the ground). Pale green to white. Long antennae. Transparent wings. Identified primarily by the pure, sustained, crystalline trill -- the purest insect tone. If you hear a clean, ringing trill on a summer night from the shrub layer, it is likely a tree cricket.

nodes.title: Pure Sustained Trill -- 98th Acoustic Element (species side)
nodes.description: One of the purest tones in nature. Nearly sinusoidal waveform. Sustained, crystalline, 2-4 kHz. E=3. Remarkable tonal clarity with minimal harmonic content. A biological tuning fork.

nodes.title: Dolbear's Law -- The Thermometer Cricket
nodes.description: Chirp rate correlates precisely with temperature. T(F) = 40 + chirps/13s. Verified biophysics. Metabolic rate drives wing-rubbing speed. The cricket IS a thermometer.

nodes.title: Gryllidae Returns -- Same Family, Different Sound World
nodes.description: Field cricket (87): dense rhythmic chirping, E=4, dry meadow. Tree cricket (98): pure sustained trill, E=3, shrub. Same family, radically different acoustic characters. Within-family diversity.

nodes.title: Shrub Habitat -- Elevated Calling Position
nodes.description: Calls from shrubs and low trees. Elevated position helps pure tone carry. Not ground-dwelling like field crickets. Different ecological niche within same family.

nodes.title: The Gold Casio Contrast -- Biological and Electronic Oscillators
nodes.description: Degree 98 pairs tree cricket with Gold Casio. Both pure, sustained tones. Biological oscillator (E=3) and electronic oscillator (E=4). Cricket as nature's synthesizer.

cross_references.relationship: SAME FAMILY GRYLLIDAE! Field cricket (87): dense rhythmic chirping, E=4, dry meadow. Tree cricket (98): pure sustained trill, E=3, shrub. Same family, radically different acoustic characters.
ORTHOPTERA CONNECTION. Both Orthoptera, different families. Katydid rasp (88, Tettigoniidae) and tree cricket trill (98, Gryllidae). Both stridulating; different textures.
INSECT SUSTAINED SOUND PARALLEL. Cicada throb (89, E=5) and tree cricket trill (98, E=3). Both sustained insect sounds. Different mechanisms and amplitudes.
INSECT ERA -- QUIET END. Both low-energy insect entries. Stonefly (94, E=2) and tree cricket (98, E=3). The quiet end of the insect acoustic spectrum.
PURE TONE PARALLEL! Both remarkably pure tones. Peeper peep (90) and tree cricket trill (98). Different phyla (amphibian vs insect), convergent acoustic purity. Nature's tuning forks.
